coredata.dc:title: Electrical properties of nitrogen-doped 4-aminomorpholine functionalized-graphene quantum dot-based heterojunction diode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dikicioğlu, Elanur
coredata.dc:description: The scientific world has recently begun to pay close attention to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) semiconductor junctions (GQDs-S), particularly because of their significant potential to open doors to new research fields, including sensing, communication, and detection. GQDs can be functionalization with various functional groups to enhance their properties. In this study, 4-aminomorpholine (4A-MORP) substituted N-doped GQDs (4A-MORP N-GQDs) were synthesized from the reaction of 4A-MORP and citric acid in aqueous medium in an autoclave. The synthesized 4A-MORP N-GQDs were characterized by ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Next, the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode was fabricated. The properties of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si Schottky diode were investigated using current-voltage (I-V) measurements in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity (at ± 3 V and 300 K). Cheung's and Norde's functions, as well as classical thermionic emission (TE) theory, were used to analyze the diode's ideality factor (n), barrier height (Φb), and series resistance (Rs). The barrier heights (Φb) of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode obtained from these approaches are 0.74 eV and 0.94 eV in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity, respectively. At ± 3 V, the diode's rectification ratios (RR) in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity are calculated to be approximately 1.02 × 102 and 1.16 × 101, respectively. According to the results obtained, the produced Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si exhibits Schottky diode behavior, and these results are expected to be instrumental in better understanding electronic technology based on functionalised graphene quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
4-Aminomorpholine (4A-MORP) functionalized N-doped GQDs
Nanocomposite diode
I-V characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles (FT-IR, UV-Vis, TEM and XPS)
Electrical properties of Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p Si structure

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS survey spectra of 4A-MORP N-GQDs (b) XPS spectra of 4A-MORP N-GQDs for O1s, N1s and C1s.
TEM image of the 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles.
Semi-logarithmic I-V characteristics of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity and in the dark.
A schematic energy band diagram of Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p-Si structure.
dV/d(ln I) vs. I and H(I) vs I characteristics of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity and in the dark.
F(V, I) from Norde’s plot of the Ag/p-Si in the dark and Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity and in the dark.
The energy-dependent profile of Nss of the Ag/p-Si and the Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity and in the dark.
Electrical parameters of the Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p-Si (Dark), Ag/p-Si (Dark), Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p-Si (Illumination - 100 mW/cm2) structures at room temperature.
Comparison of diode parameters for various types of 4A-MORP/N/GQDs Schottky diodes.

head.ce:title: Electrical properties of nitrogen-doped 4-aminomorpholine functionalized-graphene quantum dot-based heterojunction diode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yüksek İhtisas University, Vocational School of Health Services, Electroneurophysiology Program, Ankara 06800, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yüksek İhtisas University, Vocational School of Health Services, Electroneurophysiology Program

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yüksek İhtisas University, Vocational School of Health Services, Electroneurophysiology Program, 06800 Ankara, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The scientific world has recently begun to pay close attention to graphene quantum dots (GQDs) semiconductor junctions (GQDs-S), particularly because of their significant potential to open doors to new research fields, including sensing, communication, and detection. GQDs can be functionalization with various functional groups to enhance their properties. In this study, 4-aminomorpholine (4A-MORP) substituted N-doped GQDs (4A-MORP N-GQDs) were synthesized from the reaction of 4A-MORP and citric acid in aqueous medium in an autoclave. The synthesized 4A-MORP N-GQDs were characterized by ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Next, the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode was fabricated. The properties of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si Schottky diode were investigated using current-voltage (I-V) measurements in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity (at ± 3 V and 300 K). Cheung's and Norde's functions, as well as classical thermionic emission (TE) theory, were used to analyze the diode's ideality factor (n), barrier height (Φb), and series resistance (Rs). The barrier heights (Φb) of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode obtained from these approaches are 0.74 eV and 0.94 eV in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity, respectively. At ± 3 V, the diode's rectification ratios (RR) in the dark and under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity are calculated to be approximately 1.02 × 102 and 1.16 × 101, respectively. According to the results obtained, the produced Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si exhibits Schottky diode behavior, and these results are expected to be instrumental in better understanding electronic technology based on functionalised graphene quantum dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Creating new quantum dots (QD) types with tunable characteristics is a promising avenue for developing and engineering novel semiconductor devices with remarkable characteristics and capabilities. The size and shape of QDs govern their electrical and optical characteristics. QDs are noncrystalline semiconductor materials with matchless features. QDs have superior optical properties, including tunable emission spectra, broad excitation spectra, robust photostability, high fluorescence efficiency, etc., in contrast to traditional bulk materials. Between individual molecules and bulk semiconductors, the particle's qualities are crucial because of its tiny size and high surface-to-volume ratio [1–4]

These particles range in diameter from 10 to 75 at. lengths, with a range of roughly 2–15 nm. Group III-V and II-VI elements of the periodic table allow QDs to be obtained [5–9]. Almost any semiconductor metal compound may be used to produce QDs. Due to quantum confinement effects, quantum dots (QD), which are quasi-zero-dimensional nanocrystals, exhibit unique electrical, magnetic, and optical properties in contrast to macroscopic systems [10–13]. One may alter the band gap by varying the size of the quantum dots. The color of the radiation produced by QDs is likewise altered by their size change due to quantum restriction [14,15]. Making QDs radiate at all visible frequencies and even infrared light is a controlled procedure. Therefore, QDs promise major advances in electronics, computer applications, solar panels, medical imaging processes, and LEDs [9,16–20]. Carbon allotropes with high absorption, high carrier mobility, and tunable bandgap include carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, fullerenes, and GQDs [21–23].

Recent years have increased interest in carbon-based electronics, particularly those based on GQDs. New applications might result from the controlled modification of GQDs' electrical and optoelectronic characteristics through synthetic or physical/chemical procedures. It may complement or replace metal- and inorganic semiconductor-based optoelectronic devices. GQDs are zero-dimensional bulk carbon material fragments with properties similar to graphene and carbon dots. They are regarded as minute pieces of graphene [24,25]. They are obtained from several or single sheets of graphene fragments. GQD is a graphene material, the latest addition to the nanocarbon material family, characterised by an atomically thin graphite plane (1 or 2 layers, thinner than 2 nm) with lateral dimensions smaller than 10 nm, absorbance in the UV region (230–300 nm) and showing a honeycomb pattern (sp2-sp2 carbon bonds) [26–28]. GQDs are a few atoms thick and less than 30 nm in size. GQDs possess excellent features such as tunable surface edges, high surface area, small size, large diameter, conjugated π-π bond, thermal stability, good electrical conductivity, quantum confinement effects, edge effects, good photochemical stability, high mechanical strength, heteroatom doping, being soluble in a variety of solvents, functional groups that can be attached to the edges, and low toxicity. Due to its small size and high surface-to-volume ratio, the particle's properties play a crucial role between bulk semiconductors and individual molecules [29–32]. Due to its small size and high surface-to-volume ratio, the particle's properties play a crucial role between bulk semiconductors and individual molecules. GQDs have a graphene structure inside the dots, so the extraordinary properties of graphene are also preserved in GQDs [33]. These reasons give GQDs their intriguing optical, electrochemical, and electrical properties. As a result, GQDs have emerged as a popular material for biosensing [34] and bioimaging [35], photodetection [36], photovoltaics [37], light-emitting diode [38], and many similar areas [39,40] applications. Energy conversion/storage of the electronic device and performance of the electronic device can be improved by doping GQDs with heteroatoms [41].

It is promising to dope GQDs (N-doped GQDs) with heteroatoms such as nitrogen to modulate the structural, electronic, and chemical functionality of GQDs and further improve properties such as performance and energy conversion/storage of the electronic device [41]. Doping is important in semiconductors because it can significantly change the carrier density, which produces unique optical and electrical characteristics. Because nitrogen functionalization and doping change the intrinsic properties of graphene quantum dots, they are therefore thought to be very beneficial [29,41–43]. One study focused on the synthesis, applications, and characterization of nitrogen-doped graphene, a material produced via chemical vapor deposition [42]. In another study, N-doped GQDs produced in liquid solvents via laser ablation for electrochemical and photoluminescence applications were investigated [44]. The last few years have seen the start of research on GQDs-based Schottky diode structures, which are important to electronics technology. Devices with extremely quick switching speeds and low forward voltage operating capabilities are being developed. Pandit and Cho [45] reported that studied the contact characteristics of GQDs grown on n-type GaN semiconductors. This photodiode provided a very low dark current. The spectral photoresponse of the GQD/GaN photodiode was shown to include a sharp increase in sensitivity at wavelengths shorter than 375 nm. The potential of GQDs to improve deep ultraviolet light photodetection within the graphene/Si heterojunction is investigated in research by Zhang et al. [46]. The inclusion of GQDs improves both the ability of graphene and Si to absorb deep ultraviolet light and lowers the Schottky barrier between the two materials. The altered junction demonstrated remarkable performance parameters, such as high specific detectivity, significant quantum efficiency, rapid response time, and remarkable responsivity. According to Majumder et al. [47], the band alignment of GQDs facilitated effective charge carrier transfer in the presence of light and enhanced ZnO's photo-sensing characteristics. Doping nitrogen was found to enhance the samples' electrical transport characteristics, making them a strong candidate for semiconductor applications. Additionally, N-doped GQDs can be functionalized for specific purposes to further improve them. To this end, strong electron donation is achieved when amino group functionalization is achieved chemically using primary amine molecules, and this has a significant impact on the electrical characteristics of N-GQDs [48]. Aldehyde, cyano, carboxyl, amide, nitro, and isopropyl groups can all be used to functionalize GQDs. GQD nanocomposites functionalized with the amino group 4A-MORP have been synthesized for medical and biological applications [49,50]. The structure of N-doped GQDs functionalized with 4A-MORP can alter the Schottky junction's interface and diode characteristics. 4-Aminomorpholine substituted GQDs means (4A-MORP): It means that one of the groups on the side edges in GQDs is 4A-MORP. N-doped GQDs means that one of the carbons in the main ring is replaced by a nitrogen atom. 4A-MORP N-GQDs means that one of the groups on the side of the ring is 4-aminomorpholine, and there is a nitrogen atom inside the ring.

In this study, GQD nanocomposites functionalized with the 4A-MORP amino group, which has been used in medical and biological applications, were synthesized, and interface properties and the diode parameters of Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si structure, which have never been reported before, were researched using I-V measurements in illumination and dark conditions.

The chemicals were used without further purification after being purchased from commercial sources. Citric acid monohydrate (CA) and 4A-MORP were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A Perkin Elmer BX II spectrometer was used to get the infrared absorption spectra. A PG Instruments T + 80 UV–visible spectrometer was used to measure the UV–visible spectra. The morphology of 4A-MORP N-GQDs was analyzed using TEM at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The elemental compositions of 4A-MORP N-GQDs materials were studied using XPS.

In this work, 4A-MORP functionalized N-GQDs were effectively synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and 4-Aminomorpholine (4A-MORP) as carbon, nitrogen, and amino group precursors, respectively [29,49,51,52]. 6.30 g citric acid (30 mmol) and 1.02 g 4A-MORP (1 mL, 10 mmol) were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized distilled water and reacted in an autoclave for 18 hours at 200 °C. The suspension products were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 minutes after coming to room temperature, and the supernatant was collected. The 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles were washed once with ethanol and twice with deionized distilled water. Finally, the obtained 4A-MORP N-GQD nanoparticles were stored in a vacuum desiccator ( Fig. 1).

In this study, p-type Si (100) was used as the substrate while fabricating the diode. Following the cleaning procedures, a 124 nm-thick Ag (99.999 % pure) ohmic contact was created on the unpolished surface of the p-Si substrate by the thermal evaporation method. Then 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanocomposite solution was deposited onto the wafer's polished surface using a spin-coating technique. With the spin-coating technique, a 30 nm-thick thin film was obtained at a spin speed of 3000 rpm and a spin time of 30 seconds. Finally, circular Ag contacts with a thickness of 124 nm were formed using a circular metal mask (1.5 mm in diameter) on the 4A-MORP N-GQDs p-type Si via the thermal evaporation method. The structure of the 4A-MORP N-GQDs p-type Si diode is shown in Fig. 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles (FT-IR, UV-Vis, TEM and XPS)
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of the nanoparticles investigated are presented in Fig. 3. The frequencies of the characteristic functional groups in the FT-IR spectrum of 4A-MORP N-GQDs; νH2O; 3853–3740, νOH;1609, νCOOH; 3419–3207 s-br, νC-H; 2942 s, νCOO: 1738 s, νCN(pyridinic); 1699 s, νCC; 1553 s, νC-N; 1457 m, νC-O; 1408 and νC-O-C(etheric) observed at 1174, 115 and 1052 cm−1. C-H bending-ring puckering at 797 cm−1, and C-H deformation vibrations at 620 cm−1 were also observed [53,54]. In the spectrum, the characteristic vibrations do not overlap and are observed at different frequencies. This made an important contribution to the differentiation of the vibrations of the expected functional groups. According to the FT-IR results, it can be said that 4A-MORP N-GQDs were formed.

The absorption spectrum of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticle suspensions in water was made by UV-Vis spectrometer ( Fig. 4). One band and done shoulder are attributed to n-π* transitions of CN and CO at 380 nm and π-π* transitions of CC at 256 nm in the UV-Vis spectra of 4A-MORP N-GQDs. In addition, the band gap of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles was calculated as 3.26 eV from UV-Vis data [55]. For organic semiconductors, the bands defining the bandgap are usually referred to as the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). In organic semiconductors, the band gap is typically 2.5–4.0 eV [56,57], while in inorganic semiconductors the band gaps are 1–5 eV [58]. The band gap calculated for the 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticle (3.26 eV) is within the limits.

The energy values of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles, which give information about the chemical and binding environment, were determined using XPS and XPS spectra of 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanoparticles are given in Fig. 5(a), and (b). It is seen from Fig. 5(b) that the binding energies are 530, 398 and 283 eV for O, N and C, respectively [54,59,60]. The broad spectra observed for O, N and C in XPS indicate that these elements are in different bonding types in the material. That is, C-O and CO for O, N-C and N-O for N, and C-C and CC for C.

Furthermore, oxygen and carbon Auger peaks (OKL1 and CKL1) are observed at 1227 and 1000–997–530 eV in 4A-MORP N-GQDs (Fig. 5(a)). An outer shell electron interacts with an electron in a different shell in XPS, and the interacting (Auger) electron receives the energy difference from this interaction. This electron is an Auger electron and can emit radiation if the transferred energy exceeds the binding energy. The difference between the transferred energy and the binding energy determines the kinetic energy of the Auger electron. Furthermore, this energy is independent of the primary photon's hv energy. According to the XPS results, it can be said that 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanocomposites were formed and their structures were as given.

The structure of 4A-MORP N-GQDs was studied by TEM analysis as shown in Fig. 6. The TEM image of 4A-MORP N-GQDs shows that the particles form deformed spherical and porous structures with an average size of 5–22 nm and do not form agglomerates (Fig. 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical properties of Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p Si structure
ce:section.ce:para:
First, the TE theory was used to examine the electrical characteristics of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode. The current passing through a Schottky diode and the forward bias voltage (V>3kT/q) have the following relationship, per TE theory [45,46]
(1)
I =
I
0
exp
(
q V
n k T
)
[
1 − exp
(
−
q V
k T
)
]
(2)
I
0
= A
A
*
T
2
exp
(
−
q
φ
b
k T
)

In Eq. (1) and (2), q represents the electron charge, k represents the Boltzmann's constant, and T represents the temperature in Kelvin. I
0 represents the saturation current at V= 0 at the straight-line intercept of the lnI-V plot. The Richardson constant is denoted by A* (A*=32 A/cm2K2 for p-type Si). The symbols V, Φb, and A stand for applied voltage, effective barrier height, and the diode area, respectively.

The n value was calculated from the slope of the semi-logarithmic I-V plot for the linear region using Eq. (3).
(3)
n =
q
k T
d V
d ( ln
I )

Eq. (4) can be used to get Φb, as shown by
(4)
φ
b
=
k T
q
ln
(
A
A
*
T
2
I
0
)

The lnI-V characteristics of an Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode in the dark and 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity (at ± 3 V and 300 K) are shown in Fig. 7. The embedded graph in Fig. 7 shows the I-V curve of the Ag/p-Si (MS) diode in the dark. The forward bias lnI-V plot intercepts at 300 K were used to calculate the values of Φb.

The plot of lnI-V for the 4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity (P) and in the dark is shown in Fig. 7. The graph in Fig. 7 shows the rectifier lnI-V characteristic where the current increases with the increase in illumination intensity. It is remarkable that both under illumination and in the dark, the current reaches a good saturation at reversal biases. In addition, the lnI-V plots of the Ag/p-Si (MS) diodes (embedded graph) with and without the 4A-MORP N-GQDs layer are given in Fig. 7. The photo-generation of electron-hole pairs is the cause of the enhanced current under illumination. Reverse bias causes a photocurrent to grow with increased illumination intensity because the strong internal + external electric fields effectively separate the electron-hole pairs that are photogenerated in the extended depletion region of the 4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si heterojunction. In contrast, the reduced depletion region and weakened internal-external electric field result in a current under-illumination in the forward bias that is indistinguishable from the dark current. This internal electric field in the depletion region aids in the separation of pushing photogenerated electrons electron-hole towards pairs the through n-type layer and holes towards the p-type layer. Under forward bias, the external electric continues field to reduce play the barrier a height crucial and role facilitates in carrier-enhancing injection charge. The primary mechanism responsible for the increased current under illumination in the Ag/4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si diode is the formation of electron-hole pairs as a result of light absorption, which boosts charge carrier density. Here, the presence of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) enhances light absorption and charge separation. Under reverse bias, the electric field effectively separates the electron-hole pairs formed; recombination is reduced, and thus more current can flow. This reverse bias enhances the response of the diode to illumination by improving the collection of the charge carriers.

In Fig. 7, in the nonlinear region, increasing Φb leads to slower rises in current and voltage, resulting in a flatter semi-logarithmic curve. This relates to the Schottky barrier, which prevents carriers from passing through. In the linear region, greater Φb reduces reverse saturation current I0, resulting in lower currents overall. In the nonlinear region, increasing n causes slower rises in current and voltage, resulting in a flatter curve in the semi-logarithmic plot. This is connected to the diode's non-ideal behavior. In the linear zone, when n increases, the diode efficiency decreases and the series resistance increases, causing the slope to shift. Finally, the I-V characteristics of a Schottky barrier diode are shaped by two essential parameters: Φb and n. In the nonlinear area, Φb affects current levels, while n regulates the ideality and rate of current growth with voltage.

The semi-logarithmic ln(I)-V plot has an increased curvature at high currents. This is due to the current curve in the forward bias region being dominated by the series resistance (Rs) from the contact wires or the bulk resistance of the MIS structures. Two parameters, Φb and n are significant in the I-V characteristics' non-linear and linear sections. However, Rs is important in the linear region where forward bias I-V characteristics are downwardly curved.

The most basic criteria for selecting the interface layer are a high dielectric constant, the ability to passivate the surface, a controlled current-conduction mechanism, and the ability to minimize leakage current [61,62]. With the addition of the interface layer, the rectification ratio is expected to increase and the leakage current will decrease.

In this study, the purpose of adding a 4A-MORP N-GQDs interlayer between the Ag/p-Si diode is to reduce the leakage current and improve the diode performance. The addition of a 4A-MORP N-GQD interlayer changes the electrical properties of the Ag/p-Si diode by minimizing the leakage current and increasing the rectification ratio. Thus, a significantly improved performance is observed over the Ag/p-Si diode.

The addition of a 4A-MORP N-GQDs interlayer between the Ag/p-Si diode allowed us to obtain a diode with a significant decrease in the leakage current and a good rectification ratio (RR= 1.02 ×102 at ± 3 V).

However, the addition of a 4A-MORP N-GQDs interlayer between the metal/semiconductor (MS) junction may have also caused oxygen defects or interfacial reactions during deposition. As can be seen in Fig. 7, both Ag/p-Si (MS) and Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diodes show good rectifying behavior and the rectification ratios of the Ag/4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si diode (1.02 ×102) structure and the Ag/p-Si (MS) diode (1.29 ×102) structure are in the same level. In addition to preventing leakage current, the diode's layer of 4A-MORP N-GQDs increases the rectifying ratio. This condition is explained by the existence of the 4A-MORP N-GQDs layer passivating the semiconductor surface and the image force lowering the Schottky barrier height [63,64].

Rectification ratio (RR) is a parameter related to the characteristics and quality of the diodes. A rectification ratio RR is calculated as the ratio of the forward current density to the reverse current density at a certain applied voltage. The rectification ratio (RR = Iforw./Irev.) of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode was obtained as 1.02 × 102 at ± 3 V. The prepared Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si exhibits Schottky diode behavior, as demonstrated by all of these findings. Fig. 8 presented a schematic energy band diagram of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si structure.

The band alignment in the Ag/4A-MORP/N-GQDs/p-Si diode demonstrates how surface states and intermediate layers (N-GQDs) influence the Schottky barrier. These properties lead to non-ideal diode behavior, such as increased ideality factor and reverse leakage current, by forming new charge transport channels and altering the effective barrier height. Understanding and regulating these effects is crucial for increasing diode performance in practical applications. The Ag/4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si diode exhibits better rectification behavior and lower leakage currents as a result of the 4A-MORP N-GQDs layer, which passivates interface defects. Fig. 8 is consistent with conventional Schottky diode behavior, resulting in better carrier injection in the forward bias and lower currents in the reverse bias.
The formulas for n, Φb, and Rs were calculated using S.K. and N.W. Cheung's method [48]. Cheung's method is applied to the high-voltage region where the forward bias of the I-V plots is nonlinear.

This technique allows for the writing of n, Φb, Rs, and H(I) as
(5)
d V
d ( ln
I )
= n
k T
q
+ I
R
s
(6)
H
( I )
= V −
n k T
q
ln
(
I
A
A
*
T
2
)
(7)
H
( I )
= n
φ
b
+ I
R
s
The linear region of the forward bias I-V characteristics gives Φb. The dV/dlnI vs. I and H(I) vs. I plots for the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode at 300 K are shown in Fig. 9.

The TE method yielded n and Φb values of 6.7 and 0.74 eV in the dark and 3.8 and 0.94 eV in the illumination. Cheung plots of the 4A-MORP functionalized N-GQDs-based heterojunction diode is shown in Fig. 9. The slopes of these plots are used to determine the structure's series resistances Rs, and the values are 330.9 kΩ and 116.9 kΩ in the dark and 118 kΩ and 147.6 kΩ in the illumination, respectively. Using Eq. 7, the ideality factor n = 3.07 in the dark and 2.82 in the illumination are extracted from the intercept of the y-axis of the plots. If we substitute these n values into Eq. 7, Φb = 0.71 eV in the dark and Φb = 0.83 eV in the illumination. These values are consistent with the TE values.

The plot of dV/d(ln I) vs. I is linear with the slope of Rs and nkT/q y-axis interception points because Eq. (5) gives a straight line towards the bottom left of the curved area in the forward bias I-V characteristics. The value of Rs is obtained from the slope of the dV/d(lnI) vs. I curve, while the value of n is obtained from the cut-point of the curve. The H(I) vs. I curve's slope and the curve's cutoff point in Eq. (7) are used to construct Rs and Φb, respectively, using the n value from Eq. (5).

Furthermore, Norde's function technique is used to compute the values of Rs and Φb [65]. This equation can be expressed as follows,
(8)
F
V , I
=
V
γ
−
kT
q
ln
I
A
A
*
T
2
where γ is an integer greater than the value of n that was obtained. Φb is computed as follows,
(9)
∅
b
= F
V
min
+
V
min
γ
−
kT
q

Rs is defined as follows,
(10)
R
s
=
kT ( γ − n )
q
I
min
where Imin is the current that corresponds to the Vmin value and F(Vmin) is the minimum point of the F(V, I)-V plot. Fig. 10 displays the structure's F(V, I)-V plot.

The Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode structure was investigated using Norde, Cheung, and TE techniques. The calculated n, Φb, and Rs values are given in Table 1. The Φb values calculated from TE, Norde, and Cheung methods were 0.74 eV, 0.80 eV, and 0.71 eV, respectively. Also, it was found that the n values according to the TE technique and Cheung approach are approximately 6.7 and 3.07, respectively. Interface contaminants, interface states, series resistance, and non-uniform barriers are the causes of the n values greater than unity [66–68]. In addition, since different regions of the I-V graph are used in the calculations made using different approaches, the calculated n values are different from each other. Next, the values of Rs are determined by Norde's functions at 50 Ω and by Cheung's functions at 330.9 Ω and 166.9 Ω. The Rs values obtained from the Cheung approximation are larger than those obtained from the calculated Norde method. The Norde method is applied to the entire forward bias region of I-V plots, while the Cheung method is applied to a nonlinear high-voltage region of the forward bias.

The Schottky diodes' n, Φb, and Rs values are calculated, as shown in Table 1. Electrical parameters of the Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p-Si (in the dark), Ag/p-Si (in the dark), Ag/ 4A-MORP/N/GQDs /p-Si (under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity) structures at room temperature are given in Table 1. When the calculations in Table 1 are examined, it is seen that the n value of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si diode under 100 mW/cm2 illumination intensity is closer to an ideal. In other words, the Schottky barrier Φb grew while the ideality factor n decreased with increasing illumination intensity, according to the diode parameters that were determined using the TE and Cheung function method. In this study, the electrical behavior and photoresponse of the diode were investigated depending on the illumination intensity, and it was found that the device was rectifying and photosensitive.

As shown in Table 1, the electrical parameters of Ag/p-Si and Ag/4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si diode structures were compared. It is seen that the 4A-MORP N-GQDs layer brings the structure closer to the ideal state according to the Cheung method. In addition, the Io, leakage current and n (Cheng method) values for the Ag/4A-MORP N-GQDs/p-Si structure are lower than those for the Ag/p-Si structure, and the barrier height (Φb) values are consistent with each other. In addition, this 4A-MORP N-GQDs layer caused an increase in the series resistance. The larger Rs may be due to the interface layer, inhomogeneous barrier heights, space charge injection, interface states or dislocations.

The 4A-MORP N-GQDs enhance the rectifying ratio by improving charge carrier transport and by reducing recombination losses at the semiconductor interface. In addition, there is a decrease in leakage current. The N-GQDs form localized states that can trap charge carriers, which in effect reduces the Schottky barrier height at the interface with p-Si and enhances the diode’s rectifying properties.

By taking voltage-dependent n and barrier height into consideration and using the following equations, the energy-dependent profile of Nss may be derived from the I-V data at the forward bias region using the Card-Rhoderick [69] approach.
(11)
n
V
= qV / [ kT ln
I
I
0
]
(12)
∅
e
=
∅
BO
+
1 −
1
n
V
V

Furthermore, the following is the energy location of Nss for p-type semiconductors [70]:
(13)
E
ss
−
E
v
= q (
∅
e
− V )

Thus, Eqs. 11, 12, and 13 were used to extract the Nss vs (Ess-Ev) plot as follows [53,55]:
(14)
N
ss
V
=
1
q
[
ε
i
d
( n
V
− 1 ) −
ε
s
W
d
]

This is clearly shown in Fig. 11, where the value of Nss increases exponentially from the mid-gap of Si to the top of the valance band edge. The energy-dependent changes in Nss were shown to be caused by a unique density distribution between metal and semiconductor. Additionally, the plots of Nss as a function of Ess−Ev derived from the above equations are displayed in Fig. 11. As the energy differential Ess−Ev grows, the surface states show an almost exponential drop. But, with the illumination, the concentration begins to decrease. By the observed decreasing ideality factor, the light that produces electron-hole pairs at the interface also causes a drop in the surface states.

The diode characteristics for PEI‑NGQDs, 4A-MORP/N/GQDs, GdNPs-doped PEI‑NGQDs, and La(OH)3NPs-doped PEI‑NGQDs-based diodes in dark circumstances at 300 K are included in Table 2 for comparison with our study.

A comparison of diode parameters for GdNPs-doped PEI‑NGQDs [71], PEI‑NGQDs [29], La(OH)3NPs-doped PEI‑NGQDs-based diodes [72] and 4A-MORP functionalized N-GQDs-based heterojunction diode is presented in Table 2.

Berktaş et al. [29] studied the I−V characteristics and C/(G/ω) characteristics of PEI-NGQDs diodes at different frequencies (from 1 kHz to 2 MHz) and voltage ranges from −3 to + 7 V. Orhan et al. [71]studied the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and electrical properties of PEI-NGQDs doped with rare earth element Gd. When the PLQYs of the GdNPs PEI-NGQDs (35.96 %) and Gd-free PEI-NGQDs (7.60 %) samples were compared, the Gd-doped sample's quantum efficiency was 470 % greater. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that the diode exhibits ohmic behavior (RR: 14 ± 5 V) after doping, while the Gd-free PEI-NGQDs sample has a good rectification ratio (RR: 2.8 × 104, ± 5 V).
Additionally, the Gd-free PEI-NGQDs sample showed negative capacitance (NC) behaviour at low frequencies, whereas the Gd-doped PEI-NGQDs structure showed no NC behaviour at any frequency range [71].

According to the results, the undoped PEI-NGQDs diode's RR is ten times greater than the La(OH)3NPs-doped PEI-NGQDs diode's (RR = 2.8 × 103 at ± 2 V). The n value of the lanthanum doped sample was found to be closer to the ideal diode value. It was observed that there was a very small difference in barrier height (2 %) between the undoped structure and the lanthanum-doped diode.

The purpose of this study is to increase the rectification ratio and performance of the Ag/p-Si diode. Since we cannot change the parameters of the Ag/p-Si diode, an interlayer (4A-MORP/N/GQDs) has been added. The parameters of this interlayer can be changed. Thus, the rectification ratio can be improved. The functional groups of this interlayer (4A-MORP/N/GQDs) can be changed or doped with rare earth elements. In Table 2, it has been seen that the rectification ratio was improved by changing the parameters of the interlayer in previous studies. According to the results of this study, it is planned to further improve the rectification ratio of the Ag/p-Si diode by doping GQDs with new functional groups or rare earth elements in future studies.

In this work, we examined the characteristics of functionalized-GQDs/p-type Si diode, which may lead to new research areas like communication, sensing, and detection. 4A-MORP N-GQDs were successfully synthesised using the hydrothermal technique. UV–vis, FT-IR, TEM, and XPS analyses were performed to characterise the nanocomposites. The 4A-MORP N-GQDs nanocomposite was deposited on top of p-type Si to fabricate a heterojunction diode. The I-V properties of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si structure were investigated using three widely used methods in this field. The electrical behaviour and the photo-response of the device to light levels were studied and it was shown that the device is both light-sensitive and rectifier. It has been seen that the Schottky barrier Φb rises as the illumination intensity rises, while the ideality factor n decreases. The produced Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si exhibits Schottky diode behaviour, according to the experimental results obtained. This work sheds light on the production process as well as the electrical and photo-detection characteristics of the Ag/4A-MORP/N/GQDs/p-Si Schottky diode. The study's findings are predicted to have a substantial impact on our understanding of GQD-based electronic technology, as well as enabling the development of new hybrid functional materials for use in electronic or optoelectronic applications with tunable electrical properties.

elanur dikicioğlu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author would like to thank the Photonics Application and Research Center of Gazi University.
